matura_2024_01a.question_ru: An atom of a certain element has the electron configuration 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁴. Determine the period number (X) and group number (Y) in which this element is located in the periodic table.
matura_2024_01a.explanation_ru: The configuration 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁴ means 16 electrons (Z = 16, sulfur). Three shells — period 3. Group = 10 + number of s and p electrons in the outermost shell: 2 + 4 = 6, i.e. group 16 (IUPAC). Answer: X = 3, Y = 16.

matura_2024_01b.question_ru: Select two correct statements about the structure of the ⁵⁶Fe isotope atom.
matura_2024_01b.explanation_ru: ⁵⁶Fe: Z = 26 (26 protons, 26 electrons), A = 56 (30 neutrons = 56 − 26). Statement 1 is correct (26p, 30n). Statement 4 is correct — the ground-state electron configuration of iron is [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s². Statements 2, 3 and 5 are incorrect.

options.text: The nucleus contains 26 protons and 30 neutrons.
The mass number of this isotope is 26.
In the ground state, the atom has the configuration [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s².
The atom has 56 protons in the nucleus.

matura_2024_01c.question_ru:
Arrange the following elements in order of increasing first ionization energy.
  1) Na  2) Mg  3) Al
matura_2024_01c.explanation_ru: In the third period, ionization energy generally increases from left to right, but Al (3p¹) has a lower ionization energy than Mg (3s² — a full s-subshell is more stable). Correct order: Na (495.8 kJ/mol) < Al (577.5 kJ/mol) < Mg (737.7 kJ/mol), i.e. 1 → 3 → 2.

matura_2024_02a.question_ru: Match each substance with the type of chemical bond that is dominant in its structure.
matura_2024_02a.explanation_ru: NaCl — a salt, ionic bond (1). H₂O — a molecule with a polar covalent O–H bond (2). Fe — a metal, metallic bond (4). Answer: A→1, B→2, C→4.

matura_2024_02c.question_ru: Explain why the boiling point of water (100 °C) is much higher than that of hydrogen sulfide H₂S (−60 °C), even though H₂S has a greater molecular mass. In your answer, refer to the type of intermolecular interactions.
matura_2024_02c.explanation_ru: Key points: (1) water forms hydrogen bonds O–H···O, (2) sulfur is less electronegative and has a larger radius than oxygen, so H₂S does not form effective hydrogen bonds, (3) stronger intermolecular interactions = higher boiling point.

matura_2024_03a.question_ru: Calculate the molar mass of ammonium sulfate (NH₄)₂SO₄ and give the result in g/mol rounded to the nearest integer.

matura_2024_03b.question_ru: In the complete combustion of methane: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O, 8.0 g of methane was burned. Calculate the volume (in dm³) of CO₂ produced at standard conditions (STP: 1 mol of gas = 22.4 dm³). Give the result to the nearest tenth.
matura_2024_03b.explanation_ru: M(CH₄) = 16 g/mol. n(CH₄) = 8.0 / 16 = 0.5 mol. From the equation: n(CO₂) = n(CH₄) = 0.5 mol. V(CO₂) = 0.5 × 22.4 = 11.2 dm³.

matura_2024_03c.question_ru: Match each description with the appropriate stoichiometric law or concept.
matura_2024_03c.explanation_ru: A — Avogadro's law (1): equal volumes of gases → same number of molecules. B — law of conservation of mass (2): mass of reactants = mass of products. C — law of definite proportions (Proust) (3): constant ratio of element masses.

left_items.label: A
left_items.text: At the same conditions of T and p, equal volumes of gases contain the same number of molecules.

left_items.label: B
left_items.text: The mass of reactants in a chemical reaction equals the mass of products.

left_items.label: C
left_items.text: The ratio of element masses in a given chemical compound is constant.

matura_2024_04a.question_ru:
Arrange the following acids in order of increasing acid strength.
  1) HClO₄  2) HClO  3) HClO₃
matura_2024_04a.explanation_ru: The strength of oxyacids increases with the oxidation state of the central atom (more oxygen atoms enhance charge delocalization in the anion). HClO (Cl⁺¹, weakest) < HClO₃ (Cl⁺⁵) < HClO₄ (Cl⁺⁷, strongest). Order: 2 → 3 → 1.

matura_2024_04b.question_ru: Match each solution with the appropriate chemical character (pH reaction).
matura_2024_04b.explanation_ru: CH₃COONa — salt of a weak acid and a strong base → anionic hydrolysis → pH > 7 (2). NH₄Cl — salt of a weak base and a strong acid → cationic hydrolysis → pH < 7 (1). KNO₃ — salt of a strong acid and a strong base → no hydrolysis → pH = 7 (3).

matura_2024_05a.question_ru: For the reaction: 2KMnO₄ + 16HCl → 2MnCl₂ + 5Cl₂ + 2KCl + 8H₂O, determine the oxidation states of manganese in the reactant (X) and the product (Y).
matura_2024_05a.explanation_ru: In KMnO₄: K⁺¹, O²⁻, so Mn: +1 + x + 4×(−2) = 0 → x = +7. In MnCl₂: Cl⁻¹, so Mn: x + 2×(−1) = 0 → x = +2. Manganese is reduced from +7 to +2.

matura_2024_05b.question_ru: Select two processes that are examples of oxidation.
matura_2024_05b.explanation_ru: Oxidation is the loss of electrons (increase in oxidation state). Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ + e⁻ (1) — oxidation of iron. S²⁻ → S⁰ + 2e⁻ (3) — oxidation of sulfur. The remaining (2, 4, 5) are reduction (gain of electrons).

matura_2024_06a.question_ru: Match each organic compound with the appropriate functional group.
matura_2024_06a.explanation_ru: CH₃COOH — acetic acid, contains a carboxyl group −COOH (2). CH₃CHO — acetaldehyde (ethanal), contains an aldehyde group −CHO (3). C₂H₅OH — ethanol, contains a hydroxyl group −OH (1).

matura_2024_06b.question_ru: Select two compounds that will give a positive Tollens' test (silver mirror reaction).
matura_2024_06b.explanation_ru: The Tollens' test detects the aldehyde group (−CHO). Methanal (HCHO) is an aldehyde (1) — it produces a silver mirror. Glucose (3) has a hidden aldehyde group in its open-chain form. Acetone is a ketone (does not react), ethanol is an alcohol, benzoic acid is a carboxylic acid — none of them give a positive Tollens' test.

matura_2024_06c.question_ru: Write the equation of the esterification reaction of acetic acid with ethanol. Give the systematic (IUPAC) name of the resulting ester and the reaction conditions.
matura_2024_06c.explanation_ru: Esterification: carboxylic acid + alcohol ⇌ ester + water. CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O. IUPAC name: ethyl ethanoate (commonly ethyl acetate). Catalyst: concentrated H₂SO₄. The reaction is reversible — the equilibrium can be shifted toward products, e.g., by removing water.

matura_2024_07a.question_ru:
For the equilibrium reaction: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), ΔH = −92 kJ
Select two factors that shift the equilibrium toward the products (to the right).
matura_2024_07a.explanation_ru: Le Chatelier's principle: (1) Increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium toward fewer moles of gas: 4 moles of reactants → 2 moles of products, so to the right. (3) Lowering temperature favors the exothermic reaction (ΔH < 0), i.e. to the right. A catalyst (4) does not shift the equilibrium. Raising temperature (2) and decreasing pressure (5) shift it to the left.

matura_2024_07b.context_ru: In an experiment, the rate of decomposition of hydrogen peroxide was studied: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂. The volume of O₂ evolved was measured as a function of time. In experiment A, 50 cm³ of H₂O₂ solution at a concentration of 0.5 mol/dm³ was used at 20 °C. In experiment B, one condition was changed.
matura_2024_07b.question_ru: Which of the following factors could cause the gas to evolve faster in experiment B while keeping the final O₂ volume the same as in A? Give the number and justify your choice.
matura_2024_07b.explanation_ru: A catalyst (MnO₂) speeds up the reaction but does not change the amount of reactants — the final O₂ volume is the same (same number of moles of H₂O₂). Increasing the concentration (1) would increase both the rate and the final volume (more moles of H₂O₂). Reducing the solution volume (3) would give a smaller final volume.

options.text: Increasing the concentration of H₂O₂ to 1.0 mol/dm³ while keeping the volume at 50 cm³.
Reducing the solution volume to 25 cm³ while keeping the concentration at 0.5 mol/dm³.

matura_2024_08a.question_ru: Match each electrochemical process with the appropriate description.
matura_2024_08a.explanation_ru: Electrolysis of NaCl(aq): a non-spontaneous process; at the cathode: 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2OH⁻ (1). Daniell cell: conversion of chemical energy to electrical energy; Zn is the anode (more active metal) (2). Iron corrosion: a spontaneous electrochemical process that destroys the metal (3).

left_items.label: C
left_items.text: Corrosion of iron in humid air

options.text: Electrical energy is converted to chemical energy; H₂ is evolved at the cathode.
Chemical energy is converted to electrical energy; zinc is the anode.
A spontaneous electrochemical process leading to metal destruction.
Electrical energy is converted to thermal energy.

matura_2024_08b.question_ru: During the electrolysis of aqueous CuSO₄ with platinum (inert) electrodes, 3.2 g of copper was deposited at the cathode. Calculate the volume of gas (in dm³, at STP) evolved at the anode. M(Cu) = 64 g/mol; V_m = 22.4 dm³/mol. Give the result to the nearest tenth.
matura_2024_08b.explanation_ru:
At the cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. n(Cu) = 3.2/64 = 0.05 mol. Electrons transferred: 0.05 × 2 = 0.1 mol.
At the anode (inert electrodes): 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻. n(O₂) = 0.1/4 = 0.025 mol.
V(O₂) = 0.025 × 22.4 = 0.56 dm³ ≈ 0.6 dm³.

matura_2024_08c.question_ru: Explain why galvanizing (zinc coating) is an effective method of protecting iron from corrosion, even when the zinc coating is damaged (scratched). Refer to standard electrode potentials: E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V; E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) = −0.44 V.
matura_2024_08c.explanation_ru: Key points: (1) E°(Zn) < E°(Fe), so zinc acts as the anode, (2) zinc is oxidized instead of iron — this is cathodic (sacrificial) protection, (3) iron becomes the cathode and does not corrode as long as it is in contact with zinc.
